---
draft: true
title: "Supply Chain Modelling Risk"
description: "Using Data Analytics and Supply Chain Modelling to Improve Labour Governance, Climate Resilience, and Worker Welfare in India’s Brick Kilns and Global Food Supply Chains"
date: 2026-03-05
date-modified: 2026-05-28
categories: [Networks, Econometrics, Supply Chains]
image: ../assets/images/img-scatter.svg
status: in-review
---

[← Back to Projects](../projects.html){.back-link}

<div class="label" style="margin-bottom: 16px;">Networks · Under Review · Global Environmental Change</div>

## Overview

If you cannot see your supply chain, you cannot see who is being harmed in it. This project tests that claim empirically: does the structural complexity of a country's commodity imports — measured using network metrics — predict the extent to which forced labour in those imports goes undetected?

We construct a multilayer trade network for 120 countries across 85 commodity classes and compute node-level metrics (betweenness, clustering coefficient, path length) as proxies for supply chain opacity. We then regress these metrics against modern slavery prevalence estimates adjusted for detection probability.

## Methods

- **Trade network**: BACI World Trade Database (2010–2022), 120 countries, 85 HS2 commodity classes.
- **Opacity metrics**: betweenness centrality, average clustering coefficient, mean shortest path length, network density.
- **Outcome variable**: Walk Free Foundation prevalence estimates adjusted for under-reporting using a detection model.
- **Identification**: instrumental variable approach using colonial trade history as an instrument for current network position.

## Key findings

1. A one-standard-deviation increase in supply chain betweenness is associated with a 22% higher probability of forced labour going undetected.
2. The effect is strongest for agricultural commodities, weakest for manufactured goods.
3. International due-diligence legislation has a measurable but modest effect on reducing the opacity–concealment relationship.

## Status

Under review at *Global Environmental Change*. Pre-print available on request.

## Links

- [GitHub repository](https://github.com/edgarrodriguezhuerta)
- Pre-print: forthcoming
